summary: Gruber documents his setup for going completely Flash-free on Mac OS X, explaining the technical distinction between Safari plugins and extensions, and how he disabled Flash Player entirely rather than using ClickToFlash. He argues that removing Flash is more honest than blocking it, since websites can then serve alternative content. For the rare cases where Flash is needed, he uses Google Chrome's built-in Flash Player as a workaround, opening pages there temporarily and quitting when done. He also dismisses concerns about HTML5 ads eventually replacing Flash's performance problems, saying he'll deal with that when it happens.
key_points: Removing Flash entirely is preferable to ClickToFlash because it lets websites serve non-Flash alternatives instead of pretending Flash is installed
Google Chrome's self-contained Flash Player provides a clean workaround for the rare Flash content that has no HTML5 alternative
Disabling Flash eliminates crashes in non-browser WebKit apps and prevents Flash from running in background tabs
The technical distinction between Safari plugins (old Netscape-era content handlers) and Safari 5 extensions (JavaScript-based) matters for understanding how ClickToFlash works
Concerns about HTML5 animated ads replacing Flash's performance burden are premature — the gains from disabling Flash are real and available today
money_quotes: To me this is better, and in some way more honest, than using ClickToFlash. Without Flash installed, Safari effectively tells websites you visit, "Hey, I don't have Flash installed", which allows the sites to send alternative content.
With ClickToFlash, Safari is effectively telling websites you visit, "Yes, sure, I have Flash installed," but then not actually loading Flash content.
It also means that the Flash plugin never gets loaded into other non-browser apps that happen to use WebKit — eliminating the number one source of crashes for many of these apps.

So, whenever I hit a page with Flash content I wish to view, I open that page in Chrome. As soon as I'm done watching it, I quit Chrome, which ensures Flash Player isn't left running in the background.
My answer: We'll cross that bridge when we come to it. As of today, there are significant performance and battery life gains to be had by disabling Flash Player on Mac OS X.
I see far fewer "Flash missing" boxes in web pages now than I did with ClickToFlash.
key_insight: Completely removing Flash rather than merely blocking it is both more honest and more effective, because it lets websites serve proper alternatives while eliminating Flash's performance and stability costs entirely.
